‘Last year was clearly my best year. And it doesn’t faze me at all these headlines of me being title favourite because I’ve told myself over these years, positive media comments or negative media comments, or fan comments, is honestly in one ear and out the other.

‘Because the good stuff doesn’t make me drive faster and it doesn’t make me go slower and the bad stuff doesn’t make you go faster or make you go slower. So, as a professional athlete, I’ve taught myself that this is locked in a little drawer. I just focus on what I can control.’

He was third-quickest in practice on Friday, with McLaren’s Oscar Piastri fastest, Antonelli second. Hamilton was fourth. But most paddock people think Mercedes have more to offer, a belief that will be stood up or eroded in qualifying and, more so in the grand prix at a thronged Albert Park, where some informed engineers rate the Silver Arrows a second a lap faster than the rest.

The biggest embarrassment is Aston Martin’s. They are light years back. Team principal Adrian Newey is not convinced they will even make it to the end of race, blaming Honda for supplying an engine that is deficient and which he said would give his drivers, 44-year-old Fernando Alonso and the owner's son Lance Stroll, nerve damage with its vibrations.

It’s fine to point a finger at Honda to an extent, but the blame should be shared around. Newey is team principal and carries the can. He, in turn, might question owner Lawrence Stroll over why he keeps rotating staff rather than planting seeds. They are the opposite of the super-team he hoped for and on which he spent £200million in developing their fine factory at the gates of Silverstone.

Back to Russell, who said he would miss Carmen this weekend, so important is she to his sporting success. Indeed, asked if he would emulate Ferrari’s Charles Leclerc, who married his fiancee Alexandra last week, he said: ‘I mean, in the near future.

'I respect all the other drivers, but I’m not scared of any of them. And even the greatest driver who is on the grid at the moment (Max Verstappen, left) doesn’t worry me'

Testing was strong and most paddock people think Mercedes have more to offer in Melbourne, where some informed engineers rate the Silver Arrows a second a lap faster than the rest

‘Given she has appendicitis, she is gutted not to be here, not because of Melbourne, but because she’s so excited for the season. It shows me how lucky I am to have someone who cares so much about what I do and is so invested in it. That helps a huge amount in this journey to success.'

Asked how many titles he might win in his dream scenario, the heir presumptive smiled. He is careful not to count his chickens early.

But he said: ‘Who knows? The only thing I reflect on is (Michael) Schumacher won his first title with Ferrari in his fifth season, and no one realises he had four years of not winning championships.’



For the record this is George Russell’s fifth season at Mercedes.

Sports.title: Caroline Wozniacki shows off her toned body in incredible yacht holiday photos - with former world No 1 looking set to retire AGAIN at 35 after giving birth to third child last year
Sports.content:
Former world No 1 tennis player Caroline Wozniacki showed off her athletic physique in photos from a luxury yacht holiday in the Caribbean.

Wozniacki, 35, wore a red bikini as she posed on the deck in the waters of Saint Barthelemy, where she holidayed with her husband and former NBA star David Lee alongside several influencers.

The Danish star has not officially retired but has not appeared on the WTA Tour since the 2024 US Open.

Last year she welcomed her third child with Lee. The loved-up couple, who tied the knot back in June 2019 after getting engaged two years earlier, have a four-year-old daughter called Olivia, a three-year-old son called James, and now baby Max.

Despite clearly being in better shape than most of us, Wozniacki does not believe she will return to the court professionally.

'No. I think it's a no. If it were up to David, he would point out that I have my protected ranking and can play the big tournaments,' she told the Nothing Major Podcast last year.

Former world No 1 Caroline Wozniacki shared snaps of her holiday in the Caribbean

The 35-year-old showed off her athletic physique as she celebrated a friend's birthday

Wozniacki is pictured with her husband David Lee, the former NBA star with whom she has three children

'I still have a long time to decide. If I think I can get back in shape, never say never. But with three small children, it's already a full-time job.

'I have three small children. I want to travel. It's been fun doing TV and I really enjoy it. So I think that's my next step. I like to work a few weeks a year and then relax with my family. It's the perfect balance.'

Working a few weeks a year certainly sounds like an appealing balance and Wozniacki spent several days in the Caribbean celebrating the 40th birthday of designer Evelyn Menin.

It is a life of luxury with former NBA All-Star power forward Lee, who played for the New York Knicks and Golden State Warriors.

The former Australian Open winner was in a relationship with Rory McIlroy for three years before he dramatically broke off their engagement in 2014 after admitting he was not ready for marriage. Three years later he did tie the knot with current wife Erica Stoll.

In 2024, however, the five-time major champion filed to divorce Stoll after serving her with papers at their home in Florida, only to U-turn on that proposal less than a month later.

A sporting super-couple, McIlroy and Wozniacki were together between 2011 and 2013 but he ended the relationship, surprising the former Australian Open champion by saying he was not ready for marriage.

The duo had sent out wedding invitations after a New Year's Eve proposal in Sydney and Wozniacki posted: 'Happy New Year everyone! Rory and I started 2014 with a bang! I said YES!!!!'

Rory McIlroy and Wozniacki, pictured together at the Masters in 2013, shortly before the golfer broke off their engagement after wedding invitations had been sent

The sporting power couple, pictured here in 2012 at Newbury, broke up abruptly after McIlroy changed his mind on marriage

But only months later, McIlroy changed his mind. At the time, he put out a statement, reading: 'There is no right way to end a relationship that has been so important to two people.

'The problem is mine. The wedding invitations issued at the weekend made me realise that I wasn’t ready for all that marriage entails.

'I wish Caroline all the happiness she deserves and thank her for the great times we’ve had. I will not be saying anything more about our relationship in any setting.'

Their split came just months after Wozniacki posted a picture of McIlroy sleeping with his mouth open and glasses on, with sources at the time saying he was 'very upset by it'.

The tennis player captioned the photo, which she later deleted: 'Jetlag @McIlroyRory? Lol!!'

Wozniacki has since admitted to being stunned at the way in which the break-up was handled.

She previously told American journalist Graham Bensinger: 'I was shocked. I thought at least I would get a face-to-face or something, but there was nothing. It was a phone call, and I did not hear from him again. It kind of just ended.

'It was very hard because he made it very public from the start. He put out a press release and all of that so I didn't have a choice, it just got put in my face.'

Despite Wozniacki's claim that the break-up was one-sided, McIlroy said in an interview that the decision was mutual.

'It is quite a difficult time for Caroline and myself, and the statement really said it all,' he told ESPN.

'It was mutual, and we both thought it was the best for us, the best for both of us. Time to move on, and I’ve said all that I need to say.'

Sports.title: Meet the French brothers breaking new badminton ground in Birmingham
Sports.content:
The Popov brothers continue to break new ground at the YONEX All England 2026.

Very few siblings can lay claim to have played on the show courts of the hallowed arena in Birmingham side by side, both as partners and individuals.

In the second round, Christo Popov walked out to begin his match against former world champion Loh Kean Yew with his brother in a decider against the current world champion Kunlavut Vitidsarn on the court next to him.

Only a few hours later, the brothers paired up again to take on Japan’s Takuro Hoki and Yugo Kobayashi, a duo they had never beaten, in the men’s doubles.

Christo, who is one of the favourites to win the men’s singles, could not help but glance over at his brother’s match.

“My head is not 100 per cent in my match because I am looking at the match of my brother,” he said. “During warm-up and the 11 interval in my game, I was also watching and I think they played a great match.

“Then I just stayed focused on my match, I actually didn’t know if he won or not.

“You always keep in mind that your brother is next to you, and are trying to look at the score, but sometimes you are in a bad position so you really need to focus again. I think it is the same for both of us.”

As the brothers had opposite results in their singles matches, it also begs the question, what is harder to do, recover and prepare for a match after you’ve won or lost?

For one, the task is quietening the adrenaline that comes with an exhilarating win, for the other, it is all about banishing the thoughts of a loss and instead looking at the chance to go again instantly as a positive.

Christo Popov beat Loh convincingly 21-12 21-17 and explained how his recovery is now a well-oiled machine.

“My body is tired of constantly playing, but I think I also really enjoy being on court,” he said. “We have been working hard on handling those matches and handling those tournaments in a row.

“I am happy that the body is ready, we are having as much rest as we can have in between matches, in between the days.

“We have our medical team with us, our physio, who is doing an amazing job to make us recover as fast as we can.

“It is boring actually, now I will just have some time with my physio, eat lunch and then I sleep a lot between matches, it helps me a lot to put my head away and get back some focus, so I sometimes sleep because it helps me recover a lot.”

The Popov brothers' physio must be working overtime as the two have each played both singles and doubles this week and last.

Christo beat Toma Junior in the final of the German Open, while together they reached the second round of the men’s doubles.

This time, Toma’s singles journey ended at the round of 32 stage, meaning he had to pick himself up and go again after a crushing loss.

Having stormed into a lead in the first, Popov lost out 19-21 21-9 21-13 to Vitidsarn in a match that could not fail to sap energy.

He said: “It is not nice to lose, especially because I was expecting a lot in the men’s singles at this tournament.

“But I cannot regret anything because I went all in. That is the mentality I went into the men’s doubles with. I tried to be the best version of myself on the court and help Christo and cover him, and at the end he also stepped up his level in the last 10 points.

“It was really good that we both played at the same level.”

Despite dealing with his defeat, the older brother was able to take on more of the responsibility when they played in the doubles, earning a 21-10 15-21 21-19 victory to set up a meeting with the defending champions Kim Won Ho and Seo Seung Jae.

Christo is aiming to do something that has not been done since 1967 when Erland Kops won both the men’s singles and men’s doubles titles.

It would be a remarkable feat and it is why neither brother is aiming to be an example for others, only to continue living out their childhood.

“It is not about showing them, it is just about enjoying,” Toma Junior said. “We have been playing both since when we started badminton and if someone wants to play then go ahead.

“We know it is tough physically and we just accept the consequences, and we are aware of that.

“We are walking towards being 100 per cent playing both if we can.”

This is the one they all want to win. This is the YONEX All England. Secure your seats today: YONEX All England Open Badminton Championships Tickets - 2026 Schedule & Dates - AXS UK

Sports.title: Jo Butterfield and Jason Kean hail team spirit as wheelchair curling comeback continues
Sports.content:
Jo Butterfield and Jason Kean hail team spirit as wheelchair curling comeback continues

Jo Butterfield and Jason Kean beat the USA to make it two wins in a row

View Image Jo Butterfield and Jason Kean

Jo Butterfield and Jason Kean hailed their team spirit after continuing their wheelchair curling comeback mission with victory over the USA.

‌



The British pair lost their opening two matches in Cortina but have responded in the best possible manner, following up Thursday night’s triumph over Latvia with another hard-earned success.

‌



Butterfield and Kean edged to a 5-4 win against Laura Dwyer and Stephen Emt, claiming a vital two in a decisive sixth end powerplay.

‌



“I enjoyed that one, it felt like a battle,” said Butterfield, who hails from Doncaster. "Both teams were making shots to stay in it and it was about managing every end really well.

“The powerplay switched the momentum a little bit, you could see them go on the back foot and it was just about seeing it out and making sure we didn’t give them any more.

“We never like to make it easy for ourselves, we tend to battle them out. We have grown into every game.

‌



“The first one was our weakest performance, there were nerves there without a doubt.

“But we have talked well in the debrief, spoken about what we need to work on and put that into practice in every game since. Hopefully we will continue to do that.”

The two nations were locked at 2-2 at the halfway stage, with the Americans looking for their third win of the competition.

‌



But Butterfield and Kean helped each other through the nervy moments to come out on top, with a focus on enjoyment proving key.

“Jo knows what to say to me if I’m getting a bit sketchy, she’ll say ‘it’s alright, J’, and it’s the same the other way around,” said Kean, from Newcastle.

“At moments in that game she’d say ‘what am I doing’ and I’d say ‘it’s alright, you’re just drawing, you’ve got it, don’t worry’.

‌



“There’s that ownership we share as well, you own a great shot, you own a mistake, and that helps massively in the team bonding. We are not in it to blame each other, it has been a massive win all round for me this season.

“I love playing with Jo, it is a perfect partnership on the ice for me.”

The feeling is mutual, with Butterfield adding: “Team JJ is our secret sauce. Our relationship with each other gets us through when it’s tough or sailing high.

Article continues below

“We know how to encourage each other and support each other when we need it. That makes a difference.

“We enjoy it, we have fun when we are out there together and people can see that. We are loving the messages coming in when people can see us bouncing off each other.”

Sports.title: Matt Weston lifts lid on impact of stunning Olympic success at Crufts 2026
Sports.content:
Matt Weston lifts lid on impact of stunning Olympic success at Crufts 2026

The 29-year-old soared to individual and mixed team skeleton gold at Milano Cortina 2026 just weeks ago

View Image Double Olympic champion Matt Weston at Crufts. (Image: Royal Kennel Club/Beat Media)

Double Olympic champion Matt Weston has inspired a nation to take to the skeleton track this winter.

‌



But he never expected to find a 70-year-old taking on the daredevil sport.

‌



The 29-year-old soared to individual and mixed team skeleton gold at Milano Cortina 2026 just weeks ago and since then has helped British Bobsleigh and Skeleton reach over 7,000 sign ups for try-outs.

‌



There was one specific sign up that caught his eye, after seeing someone over 70 put their name down to try the sport that sees athletes charge down an ice chute at over 80 miles an hour.

The septuagenarian may not make it onto the elite programme for the French Alps 2030 but Weston - speaking from Crufts at the NEC Birmingham - admitted that any interest is good interest and is ready to show them the ropes in Bath.

"We've obviously had some amazing success in skeleton in the past, but I really feel like the nation got behind us in Italy," he said.

‌



"It's been an amazing to see the response from it. We've had over 7,000 applicants for bobsleigh and skeleton alone.

"It's crazy to be part of that legacy because the programme doesn't continue unless we have new athletes signing up and seeing if they could be the next Olympic champion.

"There is obviously an age range of what we're looking for but one of the sign ups was someone who is over 70, which is amazing.

‌



"I don't think they're going to quite get on the team but something like that to me is really cool and kind of funny.

"I want to get them down to trial and just have fun and try it.

"I'm hoping that they misspelled 17 but a 70-year-old on the track would be great."

‌



Weston's success saw him become a household name overnight as Team GB's first ever male Olympic skeleton champion and now the joint most successful British Winter Olympian alongside fellow slider Lizzy Yarnold.

It has sent him on a media frenzy that included a personal rendition of Happy Birthday by mezzo-soprano Katherine Jenkins just days ago.

With a day out at Crufts 2026 enjoyed and a trip to Ascot lined up, Weston is undoubtedly making the most of every opportunity that winning an Olympic medal brings with it.

‌



But the life of an elite athlete always comes back knocking, with double gold now turning into double surgery as he finally allows his body to recover.

"My body's hurting a lot at the moment still," he said. "I've lined up a shoulder and possible hip operation, which I had been hanging onto until after the Games to sort out medically."

Weston has dominated the skeleton circuit over the past few seasons, claiming the world, European and World Cup titles in the last 12 months alone.

‌



And while the fun continues off the track this summer, Weston is not fully ignoring the work he puts in on it. After all, there are always more gold medals to be won.

"I don't really have a plan at the moment, I'm kind of just seeing what I fancy doing," he said.

"I took myself on a run this morning and I regretted it instantly. We're not endurance athletes, I just sprint for a very short time but I thought I'll just see how it feels.

Article continues below

"My training's usually very rigid and I'm very organised with that sort of stuff, so for me to just rock up and do what I fancy is nice. I enjoy training, so it's not a chore for me."

Crufts, run by The Kennel Club, is a unique celebration of happy, healthy dogs and of the loving relationship that they enjoy with their owners. Crufts is held from 5-8 March 2025 at the NEC Birmingham. Viewers can follow all the action and highlights on Channel 4 and More4, and for further information and tickets visit crufts.org.uk

Sports.title: WWE legend Dustin Rhodes misses out at Crufts alongside beloved Mastiff 'Beast'
Sports.content:
WWE legend Dustin Rhodes misses out at Crufts alongside beloved Mastiff 'Beast'

The 56-year-old, best known for his character Goldust, brought his 114kg pooch, aptly named Beast, to the NEC and watched as the colossal canine finished third in the working group open category

View 2 Images Dustin Rhodes says Beast likes to feast and the 250-pound giant scoffs $250 of food each month, with eight cups a day of dry food, chicken, rice and steak (Image: Royal Kennel Club/BeatMedia)

WWE legend Dustin Rhodes swapped the wrestling ring for the parade ring by entering his prized Mastiff at Crufts – but was left frustrated at being denied a championship belt.

‌



The 56-year-old, best known for his character Goldust, brought his 114kg pooch, aptly named Beast, to the NEC and watched as the colossal canine finished third in the working group open category.

‌



Rhodes, the son of WWE icon Dusty Rhodes and brother of Wrestlemania star Cody Rhodes, has made an unexpected career U-turn in the past two years.

‌



While still an active wrestler on the AEW circuit, he enters Beast at dog shows across America and the four-legged giant has become the number one-ranked Mastiff in the country.

View 2 Images Dogs in the working group are meant to assist and protect their owners (Image: Royal Kennel Club/BeatMedia)

Rhodes says Beast likes to feast and the 250-pound giant scoffs $250 of food each month, with eight cups a day of dry food, chicken, rice and steak.

‌



Dogs in the working group are meant to assist and protect their owners but after a disappointing result, it was the judges who needed their wits about them with Rhodes confident his was the winner.

Rhodes’ ‘finishing move’ was called shattered dreams but at the NEC, it was only he who left disappointed.

“I thought our dog was the best,” he said. “I am biased but I thought our dog was amazing, it is a once-in-a-decade dog. Beast is perfect in my eyes, he weighs more than I do and he thinks he is a lap dog.

‌



“He just jumps on you and he is the best dog in the world. I would never own another breed. I can’t wait to go and see him again, I gave him a hug after he finished third. He did well, I am very pleased and happy to be over here and honoured to be at Crufts, the world’s greatest dog show.”

Rhodes is not done wrestling just yet and is due back on tour in three weeks after six months out following double knee surgery.

He had to be lively on his feet at the NEC and keep out of sight while Beast competed as the dog gets over-excited when it sees him.

Article continues below

“The day has been long, I have not seen my dog in four months,” he added. "Beast is really good with seeing somebody who has high blood pressure or low blood pressure, and we’ll go check them out. He’s a very smart dog.

"Mastiffs are amazing, amazing animals, man. I would never own another breed besides a Mastiff, and I’ve had all kinds of dogs over my life and Mastiffs are just everything – pure, loyal, loving, just crawling on your lap, hurt your bones."

Crufts, run by The Kennel Club, is a unique celebration of happy, healthy dogs and of the loving relationship that they enjoy with their owners. Crufts is held from 5-8 March 2025 at the NEC Birmingham. Viewers can follow all the action and highlights on Channel 4 and More4, and for further information and tickets visit crufts.org.uk

Sports.title: Cristiano Ronaldo leaves Saudi Arabia as Al-Nassr boss issues worrying update
Sports.content:
Cristiano Ronaldo leaves Saudi Arabia as Al-Nassr boss issues worrying update

Cristiano Ronaldo suffered an injury during Al-Nassr's last fixture and his manager, Jorge Jesus, has now revealed that the problem is worse than they initially feared

View 2 Images Cristiano Ronaldo has flown to Madrid (Image: Yasser Bakhsh/Getty Images)

Al-Nassr manager Jorge Jesus has confirmed that Cristiano Ronaldo has travelled to Spain in order to recovery from a hamstring injury. Ronaldo, 41, was seen using an ice pack after being substituted in Al-Nassr's last match, a 3-1 victory over Al-Fayha to reaffirm their lead at the top of the Saudi Pro League.

Jesus' side were due to face Dubai-based club Al-Wasl on Wednesday but the AFC Champions League clash was postponed due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East. Al-Nassr will be back in action on Saturday when they host Neom, but Ronaldo won't play.

At his pre-match press conference, Jesus explained: "In the last match, the player left while complaining of a muscle injury. After medical examinations, it became clear that the injury was more serious than expected and requires rest and recovery.

"Cristiano traveled to Spain, like other players who went for treatment when they were injured. His injury required treatment in Madrid with his personal therapist, and we hope he returns quickly and helps the team."

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! Latest news, analysis and much more on Mirror Football's Facebook page

It comes after Ronaldo's private jet was had flown to Madrid on Monday night. The Portugal captain will be keen to recover in time for the international break, as his country finalise their preparations for the World Cup.

Ronaldo, who's under contract with Al-Nassr until June 2027, only missed two games through injury last season and prides himself on his extraordinary fitness. But retirement isn't far away, with the five-time Ballon d'Or hinting that he'll hang up his boots 'soon'.

In November, Ronaldo stated: "I feel very good in this moment. I score goals, I still feel quick and sharp. I'm enjoying my game in the national team. But of course, let's be honest. What I mean by soon is probably one or two years."

Should Cristiano Ronaldo retire after the World Cup? Have your say in the comments section.

View 2 Images Jorge Jesus will be without Cristiano Ronaldo on Saturday (Image: Yasser Bakhsh/Getty Images)

Last month, Ronaldo scored the 965th goal of his career. The former Manchester United superstar is bidding to reach 1,000 before he retires, but it won't change how he views his career, according to Portugal boss Roberto Martinez.

On the Stick to Football podcast, Martinez said: "They speak about the 1000 goals. He's not bothered about 1,000 goals. And he feels. 'What's the difference for me scoring 1050 or 950? I'm the same player and now he's become a bit different in the final third'."

Sports.title: Man Utd handed £43m transfer problem as club chief gives Andre Onana verdict
Sports.content:
Man Utd handed £43m transfer problem as club chief gives Andre Onana verdict

Andre Onana is on loan at Trabzonspor from Manchester United and has a valuation of between £40m and £43m

View 2 Images Andre Onana left Man Utd last summer to join Turkish side Trabzonspor on loan (Image: Seskim Photo/MB Media, Getty Images)

Manchester United have been informed by Trabzonspor that they won't pay between £40million and £43m for Andre Onana this summer. Onana arrived at Old Trafford from Inter Milan in July 2023 for £43.8m with a potential £3.4m in add-ons based on performance and achievements.

‌



However, a series of inconsistent displays and a run of errors forced United back into the market for a new goalkeeper during the summer.

‌



Senne Lammens ultimately arrived from Royal Antwerp for £18m, and that deal resulted in Onana joining Trabzonspor on loan for the remainder of the campaign.

‌



Whilst Onana has featured 21 times for the Super Lig side so far this season, they have no plans to meet United's asking price of £40m to £43m this summer., reports the Manchester Evening News.

Harry Kane dealt injury blow in bid to break Bundesliga record with Bayern Munich READ MORE :

Man Utd increase season ticket prices AGAIN as fans face hike for fourth year running READ MORE :

“As the figure from Manchester United for the purchase of Andre Onana is in the range of €45-50m, our president has sincerely informed the community in line with Trabzonspor’s realities,” Trabzonspor vice-president Zeyyat Kafkas told Gunebakıs.

FOLLOW OUR MAN UTD FB PAGE! Latest United news and more on our dedicated Facebook page

‌



“I don’t think this information affected Onana at all. Because Onana’s thinking is that if it’s not England, he wants to continue at a club in Europe.

“His family thinks the same way. The president also wanted to explain Trabzonspor’s reality. However, if the conditions change, the situation changes.”

It was recently suggested that Onana is determined to fight for his place as United's No. 1 next season and believes he will be given the opportunity to do so.

‌



Trabzonspor's manager, Fatih Tekke, stated that the Cameroon international will make his own choice regarding his future.

View 2 Images Andre Onana has featured 21 times for Trabzonspor this season (Image: Gerrit van Keulen/Soccrates Images/Getty Images)

He said: “Onana is on loan with us. There have been matches where he didn’t play well. There have also been many matches where he played well.

‌



“I’m very pleased with Onana’s positivity, not only in terms of performance but also in terms of character. You have transferred from one of the best teams in the world and you have experienced problems. He feels very comfortable here.

“The chairman’s words reflect Onana’s own thoughts. But this can sometimes change in a match. He is very happy here, but he will make his own decision about his future.”

Sports.title: Michael Ballack breaks down in heartbreaking video as Chelsea icon speaks out on son's death
Sports.content:
Michael Ballack breaks down in heartbreaking video as Chelsea icon speaks out on son's death

Emilio Ballack died in a quad bike accident aged 18 in 2021 and former Chelsea and Germany midfielder Michael has spoken out about his teenage son's passing for the first time

View 2 Images Michael Ballack has spoken about the death of his son (Image: Sky Germany/Instagram)

Former Chelsea and Germany midfielder Michael Ballack has emotionally spoken out on the death of his son. Emilio, aged 18, died after a quad bike accident near the family’s holiday home in Portugal back in 2021.

‌



The teenager was the middle of Ballack’s three sons, with older brother Louis one year older and younger sibling Jordi born three years after Emilio.

‌



In what are his first public comments on Emilio's passing, Ballack has spoken about the ‘unimaginable’ emotions he feels. During an interview spoken in his native language with Sky, titled ‘My Story’, he was asked about the impact of the loss.

‌



Clearly emotional, Ballack took a long pause before simply saying: "Difficult." He then paused again before repeating the word.

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! Latest news, analysis and much more on Mirror Football's Facebook page

A visibly distraught Ballack added: "You can't imagine it. You can't even describe it in words.

‌



“It's a process of repression. Everyone deals with it differently. I can hardly talk about him at all. I'd like to say much more, but it overwhelms me emotionally.

“That's why it's a process of denial, where you try to cope through everyday life, through many things that motivate you – work, family, your other sons.

“Of course, you love your children more than anything and want to give them as much stability, warmth, and security in life as possible. That's the responsibility you bear as a parent.

‌



“Such a loss naturally intensifies that even more. Of course, you then have greater fears. What else could happen?”

View 2 Images Michael Ballack won the Premier League with Chelsea (Image: Chelsea FC via Getty Images)

Later in the interview, Ballack was asked how his perspective on life has changed, replying: “Life is precious. That the life we lead is absolutely privileged.

‌



"That luck and misfortune also play a major role. Fate. You can call it what you want, but things just happen.

“Despite all the talk of fate, I naturally want to face it. That gives me strength again, this task, and at the same time, the realisation that life goes on.”
